1.synthesis: Peter calls the community to a double movement: strip off and crave. 'So put away all malice and all deceit and hypocrisy and envy and all slander' (v.1) — the five vices are precisely those that corrode community life; they are the social face of the old nature that must be shed like a garment. Then the positive: 'Like newborn infants, long for the pure spiritual milk, that by it you may grow up into salvation — if indeed you have tasted that the Lord is good' (vv.2–3). The craving is not general appetite but specific, even urgent — the infant analogy stresses both the intensity of the longing and the naturalness of dependence for those born again through the living word (1:23).
1.historical_context: Peter's phrase logikon adolon gala — 'pure spiritual milk' — is often translated 'pure milk of the word,' but the adjective logikon carries both 'rational/pertaining to the word' and 'spiritual/pertaining to the Logos.' The word is not synonymous with pneumatikon (spirit-ual) but with reasonable, word-grounded nourishment. The echoing of Psalm 34:8 in v.3 — 'taste and see that the LORD is good' — creates a deliberate wordplay in Greek: chrēstos (good, kind) and Christos (Christ) were nearly homophonous. Pagans sometimes confused the two words; Peter uses the resemblance to make a theological point: the Lord who is chrēstos is Christ.
1.literary_structure: The section is transitional: the vice-list of v.1 rounds off the ethical implications of the new birth announced in 1:22–25, and the craving of v.2 points forward to the christological section of vv.4–10. The structure is preparatory stripping (v.1) → active craving (v.2) → christological grounding of that craving (v.3). The 'if indeed you have tasted' is not a condition of uncertainty but an argument from experience: because you have already tasted the Lord's goodness, you know what you are craving — you are not being asked to desire something unknown.
1.theological_application: Gill reads the stripping of v.1 as directed specifically against community vices: 'all malice, or wickedness of any sort, but especially that which is prejudicial to the peace and comfort of the saints; all deceit in trading, in conversation, and in religious things; and hypocrisy, in pretending to more religion and grace than is really had.' The point is not general morality but the specific sins that destroy the fellowship. On the milk: Gill notes that 'the word of God is compared to milk, as it is suitable food for babes in Christ; it is pure and incorrupt, free from the mixture of human inventions; and it is nourishing, and causes spiritual growth.' Matthew Henry emphasizes that the growth Peter desires is 'not merely in knowledge but into salvation' — the telos of spiritual feeding is eschatological, not merely didactic. The craving is the sign that birth has occurred; growth toward salvation is the sign that craving is being satisfied.
1.christology: The Lord who is chrēstos in v.3 is Christ. Peter's citation of Psalm 34:8 applies the divine epithet of the Psalm's LORD to Jesus: the one whom they have 'tasted' in the new birth, through the preached word, through forgiveness and hope, is the same one who is the living stone to whom they will come in vv.4–5. The taste of v.3 presupposes the new birth of 1:23: those born again through the living and abiding word have already tasted what they are being urged to crave more of. The craving is not the beginning of relationship with Christ but its intensification — newborn infants do not just tolerate milk, they cry for it, and those born of the word should cry for the one whom the word reveals.

4.pericope_label: The Living Stone and a Chosen People
4.synthesis: Peter shifts from individual craving to corporate construction: 'As you come to him, a living stone rejected by men but in the sight of God chosen and precious, you yourselves like living stones are being built up as a spiritual house, to be a holy priesthood, to offer spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God through Jesus Christ' (vv.4–5). The stone-testimonia follow: Zion's cornerstone (Isa 28:16), the stone the builders rejected (Ps 118:22), and the stone of stumbling (Isa 8:14) — precious and honored to those who believe, a stone of stumbling and rock of offense to those who disobey (vv.6–8). Then the identity declaration: 'But you are a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people for his own possession, that you may proclaim the excellencies of him who called you out of darkness into his marvelous light. Once you were not a people, but now you are God's people; once you had not received mercy, but now you have received mercy' (vv.9–10).
4.historical_context: Peter conflates three stone-passages that the early church read as a cluster. Isaiah 28:16's Zion-cornerstone is explicitly cited in v.6; Psalm 118:22's rejected-and-vindicated stone appears in v.7; Isaiah 8:14's stone of stumbling in v.8. The same cluster appears at Romans 9:33 and in Jesus's own teaching (Luke 20:17–18), indicating a shared early Christian reading of the stone passages as christological. The identity declaration of vv.9–10 applies Exodus 19:6 ('kingdom of priests, holy nation') and Hosea 1:6, 9 / 2:23 ('not my people' → 'my people') to the predominantly Gentile recipients — a dramatic reapplication of Israel's covenant titles to the new community.
4.literary_structure: The section moves from the individual coming (v.4) to the corporate building (v.5) to the scriptural warrant for the stone imagery (vv.6–8) to the community's identity declaration (vv.9–10). The two outcomes of the stone — honor for believers, stumbling for unbelievers — are not arbitrary: they are 'appointed' (v.8), which frames the section's account of rejection and election within divine sovereignty. The identity declaration of vv.9–10 is the positive corollary: those who have come to the living stone are themselves becoming a spiritual house, and their corporate identity is defined by six stacked titles, all pointing toward the purpose of proclamation.
4.theological_application: Gill on the spiritual sacrifices of v.5: 'These are prayer and praise, or the sacrifices of prayer and thanksgiving; and the preaching of the word, and the ministration of ordinances; and deeds of beneficence and charity, and particularly alms; and, above all, the sacrifice of the heart and a contrite spirit.' The priesthood of all believers is not merely a Reformation doctrine but Peter's own: every member of the spiritual house has direct priestly access through Jesus Christ. Matthew Henry on vv.9–10 notes that the contrast ('once you were not a people / now you are God's people') is a summary of the gospel: 'Their change from being no people to being God's people, and from having no mercy shown to them to having received mercy, is entirely the work of God's free grace.' The marvel is not that they are now gathered but that they were ever gathered at all — and the gathering was by call, not by qualification.
4.christology: The 'living stone' is Christ in his resurrection: not merely a chosen stone but a living one, killed by the builders and raised by God. The cornerstone the builders rejected has become the foundation of the entire spiritual edifice — which means that all who come to him as living stones derive their own livingness from his. Gill observes that the stone is precious and honored 'in the sight of God': God's appraisal runs directly against the builders' rejection, and God's appraisal is vindicated in the resurrection. The six-fold identity of vv.9–10 — chosen race, royal priesthood, holy nation, people for his own possession, those called out of darkness, those who have received mercy — is the community's identity because it is first Christ's: he is the chosen one (v.4), he is the true priest, he is the holy one, he is God's own Son. The community's identity is derivative and participatory.

11.synthesis: 'Beloved, I urge you as sojourners and exiles to abstain from the passions of the flesh, which wage war against your soul' (v.11). Keep your conduct among the Gentiles honorable, so that when they speak against you as evildoers, they may see your good deeds and glorify God on the day of visitation (v.12). Submit to every human institution for the Lord's sake — to the emperor as supreme, to governors as sent by him to punish evil and praise good (vv.13–14). For this is the will of God, that by doing good you should silence the ignorance of foolish people — as free people, but not using your freedom as a cover-up for evil. The fourfold imperative: 'Honor everyone. Love the brotherhood. Fear God. Honor the emperor' (v.17).
11.historical_context: The identities paroikoi and parepidēmoi — resident aliens and sojourners — were concrete social-legal categories in the Roman world: people without full citizenship rights, without the legal protections citizens enjoyed, often subject to suspicion and slander. Peter uses them both literally (many of his recipients were social outsiders) and theologically (all believers are aliens whose true citizenship is elsewhere). The slander of v.12 likely reflects real accusations against Christian communities — antisocial behavior, refusal to participate in civic religion. The fourfold honor-structure of v.17 places the emperor in fourth position, bracketed by the universal honor due all people and the supreme fear due God — a subtle subordination of imperial claims.
11.literary_structure: The section has two movements: the private ethic (vv.11–12) and the public-institutional ethic (vv.13–17). The private movement begins with identity (sojourners, exiles) and its implication (abstain from fleshly passions). The public movement grounds submission not in the emperor's dignity but in the Lord's will — 'for the Lord's sake' (v.13). The section closes with the fourfold imperative of v.17, which is not a hierarchy but a set of discrete claims: universal dignity (honor everyone), community solidarity (love the brotherhood), ultimate allegiance (fear God), proper civic order (honor the emperor). Each is given in full without qualification from the others.
11.theological_application: Gill on the passions of the flesh that wage war against the soul: 'The flesh, or the corrupt nature of man, lusts against the spirit … these are the enemies that war against the soul, and endanger its peace and comfort here, and its eternal welfare hereafter.' The war-imagery is significant: abstaining from these passions is not a withdrawal from battle but a refusal to fight on the enemy's terms. On v.12, Matthew Henry notes the evangelical strategy embedded in the ethics: 'A Christian's good works are visible; they are to be seen of men; but not with an eye to the praise of men.' The visibility of good works is not for self-glorification but for the eschatological purpose of producing Gentile praise to God — the opponent-spectators become witnesses who, on the day of visitation, glorify the God they once opposed. Gill on v.15 sees the silencing of accusers as a present and progressive work: 'so as to put them to shame, to make them blush and be confounded, and be ashamed of their calumnies and reproaches.'
11.christology: The 'day of visitation' of v.12 (Greek hēmera episkopēs) echoes Luke 19:44 and refers to the eschatological appearing of Christ in judgment and salvation. The Gentiles who now speak against believers will, on that day, see the good deeds they witnessed and glorify God. This is a striking claim: the purpose of present holy conduct among hostile observers is their future eschatological glorification of God — suggesting that even opposition can become, through the witness of patient godliness, the occasion of conversion or at least of eschatological recognition. The one who comes on the day of visitation is the Chief Shepherd who will appear in glory (5:4).

18.synthesis: Peter addresses household servants directly: 'Servants, be subject to your masters with all respect, not only to the good and gentle but also to the unjust. For this is a gracious thing, when, mindful of God, one endures sorrows while suffering unjustly' (vv.18–19). Suffering for doing good, not for wrongdoing, is charis para theō — grace before God (v.20). The christological grounding follows immediately: 'For to this you have been called, because Christ also suffered for you, leaving you an example, so that you might follow in his steps' (v.21). Peter then weaves Isaiah 53 through the description: he committed no sin, no deceit was found in his mouth; when reviled he did not revile in return; when he suffered he did not threaten, but continued entrusting himself to him who judges justly (vv.22–23). The atonement declaration: 'He himself bore our sins in his body on the tree, that we might die to sin and live to righteousness. By his wounds you have been healed. For you were straying like sheep, but have now returned to the Shepherd and Overseer of your souls' (vv.24–25).
18.historical_context: The term hypogrammon in v.21 — often translated 'example' — is more precisely a tracing-copy: a writing tablet with letters inscribed for children to trace over as they learn to write. Christ is the original; the believer's life is the tracing. The phrase charis para theō (v.19–20) is not the common Greek word for approval (epainos) but specifically grace — suggesting that the endurance of unjust suffering is itself a gracious gift from God, not merely a commended act. The catena of Isaiah 53 in vv.22–25 is one of the most concentrated uses of the Servant Song in the NT, covering Isa 53:4 (bore our sins), 53:5 (by his wounds healed), 53:6 (straying sheep), 53:9 (no sin, no deceit), and 53:12 (bearing sins) in four verses.
18.literary_structure: The section has two inseparable movements: example and atonement. The hypogrammon of v.21 introduces Christ as pattern for unjust suffering, and vv.22–23 describe that pattern (no sin, no retaliation, entrusting to God). But vv.24–25 pivot to what the example-giver did for those who are to follow: he bore sins, he healed by wounds, he gathered straying sheep. Gill insists these two movements cannot be separated: Christ's patience is both the model for suffering servants and the sacrifice for sinning sheep, and any reading that makes him only the one (ethical exemplar) and not the other (atoning substitute) distorts the passage. The section closes not with a call to imitate but with a declaration of identity — you have returned to the Shepherd and Overseer of your souls.
18.theological_application: Gill on v.24 is explicit that the passage must not be reduced to ethics: 'He himself, and not another, bare our sins; not by way of knowledge, as some say, or by lamentation and sorrow; nor by example, as some also say … but by bearing the punishment of them, the sins themselves being imputed to him, and he standing in the room and stead of sinners.' The substitution is penal and representational: Christ does not merely model how to endure unjust punishment but actually takes the punishment that belongs to others. Matthew Henry on the 'entrusting to him who judges justly' (v.23): this is the posture of Christ under unjust condemnation — not protest, not retaliation, but the active commitment of his cause to the only just Judge. Gill notes this is precisely the posture Peter calls the servants to, by analogy: those who suffer unjustly at a master's hands are not without a court of appeal, but the court is heavenly, not earthly.
18.christology: The pairing of example and atonement in this section is one of 1 Peter's distinctive christological contributions. Christ is the hypogrammon — the tracing-copy whose pattern of patient, God-trusting endurance is to be reproduced in the life of every suffering servant. But he is also the one who 'bore our sins in his body on the tree' — a substitutionary atonement statement whose personal force ('he himself') and bodily concreteness ('in his body') make clear that the sinners' debt was discharged by the Servant himself. The letter thus holds together two christological truths that are sometimes divided: Christ is both the atoning sacrifice who takes away sin and the suffering servant who shows how sin-freed people live. The Shepherd and Overseer of souls who receives back the straying sheep (v.25) is the same one who bore those sins — the welcome home is made possible by the cross that paid for the straying.
